coredata.dc:description: Heterogeneous Co-based materials are well-known for their exceptional catalytic properties in activating peroxymonosulfate (PMS) for the degradation of antibiotic pollutants. However, the potential applications of these materials are often hindered by significant Co nanoparticle aggregation and ion leaching. In this study, a carbon quantum dot-mediated self-assembly strategy was used to fabricate three-dimensional porous sponge-like Co@CDs-x (x represents the calcination tempeatures) catalysts with spatially confined Co nanoparticles. Among the samples, Co@CDs-900 exhibited a remarkable 97.8 % removal efficiency of TC in 11 minutes, with an apparent rate constant of 0.313 min−1. The exceptional performance of Co@CDs-900/PMS can be attributed to its large specific surface area, unique porosity, and abundant catalytically active sites, which generate ample reactive oxygen species for TC degradation. The catalyst demonstrated good degradation capabilities across a wide pH range of 3–11 and various organic pollutants such as tetranitrophenol, oxytetracycline, ciprofloxacin, methyl orange, and rhodamine B. Moreover, Co@CDs-900 maintained high activity even after the 5th cycle due to its magnetic property and spatial confinement effect, preventing catalyst loss and ion leaching during recycling tests. The degradation mechanism involved both free-radical and non-free radical pathways, with SO4 •− and 1O2 identified as the primary reactive oxygen species. The degradation intermediates of TC were identified and a plausible degradation pathway was proposed. Toxicity assessment revealed that the Co@CDs-900/PMS system effectively reduced the toxicity of TC post-degradation. This study presents an effective approach for developing sponge-like CDs assemblies with spatially confined metal nanoparticles for activating PMS in antibiotic degradation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots assemblies integrated with spatial confined Co nanoparticles for antibiotic degradation via peroxymonosulfate activation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610068, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heterogeneous Co-based materials are well-known for their exceptional catalytic properties in activating peroxymonosulfate (PMS) for the degradation of antibiotic pollutants. However, the potential applications of these materials are often hindered by significant Co nanoparticle aggregation and ion leaching. In this study, a carbon quantum dot-mediated self-assembly strategy was used to fabricate three-dimensional porous sponge-like Co@CDs-x (x represents the calcination tempeatures) catalysts with spatially confined Co nanoparticles. Among the samples, Co@CDs-900 exhibited a remarkable 97.8 % removal efficiency of TC in 11 minutes, with an apparent rate constant of 0.313 min−1. The exceptional performance of Co@CDs-900/PMS can be attributed to its large specific surface area, unique porosity, and abundant catalytically active sites, which generate ample reactive oxygen species for TC degradation. The catalyst demonstrated good degradation capabilities across a wide pH range of 3–11 and various organic pollutants such as tetranitrophenol, oxytetracycline, ciprofloxacin, methyl orange, and rhodamine B. Moreover, Co@CDs-900 maintained high activity even after the 5th cycle due to its magnetic property and spatial confinement effect, preventing catalyst loss and ion leaching during recycling tests. The degradation mechanism involved both free-radical and non-free radical pathways, with SO4 •− and 1O2 identified as the primary reactive oxygen species. The degradation intermediates of TC were identified and a plausible degradation pathway was proposed. Toxicity assessment revealed that the Co@CDs-900/PMS system effectively reduced the toxicity of TC post-degradation. This study presents an effective approach for developing sponge-like CDs assemblies with spatially confined metal nanoparticles for activating PMS in antibiotic degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are extensively used in medicine, agriculture, farming, and animal husbandry; however, their excessive presence in the environment poses significant risks to human health and ecological safety [1–4]. Tetracycline (TC), the highest-selling veterinary drug globally, is a common treatment for infections [5,6]. Nonetheless, TC is challenging to biodegrade due to its structural stability and long half-life [7,8]. It is frequently detected in aquatic environments as a result of overuse, improper disposal, and excretion [6]. Residual sub-lethal doses of TC in aquatic ecosystems may lead to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria generation. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop efficient methods for degrading TC in these environments [9–12]. Various methods such as adsorption [4], biodegradation [13], and catalytic oxidation [14] have demonstrated effectiveness in controlling and eliminating TC pollutants. Among these methods, advanced oxidation processes are considered one of the most promising technologies for wastewater purification, owing to their long half-life, high redox potential, and wide pH tolerance [12,15]. In particular, the sulfate-based advanced oxidation processes are widespread utilized for effective wastewater treatment due to their high stability, ease of operation, and lack of requirement for external energy input [16]. Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) has attracted increasing interest for its efficient degradation of organic pollutants, as its asymmetric structure requires only a small amount of energy for activation.

Previous studies have shown that homogeneous Co(II)/PMS systems exhibit catalytic activity for generating highly efficient reactive oxygen species (ROS) aimed at the removal of organic pollutants [17–19]. However, the practical application of these homogeneous systems is impeded by challenges related to their reuse [20]. Consequently, various heterogeneous Co-based materials, such as metals, oxides, sulfides, phosphides, and nitrides, have been synthesized to enhance catalytic performance in terms of both activity and reusability for PMS activation [21,22]. Metallic Co has demonstrated excellent performance in triggering multiple-valence-state redox cycles for PMS activation and degrading organic pollutants. However, the issue of Co ion leaching remains a significant challenge that requires further attention. To mitigate this problem, carbon materials are commonly used to stabilize metallic Co and enhance the metal-carbon interaction, thereby improving the stability of metallic Co and reducing ion leaching. Currently, various carbon materials, including commercial activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, and synthetic carbons such as porous carbons and carbon nanotubes derived from pyrolyzing different precursors, are under investigation. Although there has been some improvement in the stability of Co-based carbon materials, further enhancements are still necessary concerning catalyst reusability and metal ion leaching. Zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) have gained recognition since their discovery in 2004, showing diverse applications [23,24]. Due to their good biocompatibility, water solubility, chemical stability and low toxicity, they are widely used in biomedicine [25–28], environmental monitoring [29], catalyzing chemical reactions [30], medical diagnosis, and biosensing [31]. In the catalytic field, the incorporation of CDs into composite materials can enhance active edges, surface area, and structural stability, thereby improving catalytic performance. The self-assembly of CDs into a carbon sponge enhances the exposure of active sites and mass transport in catalytic processes. The dispersion of metal Co nanoparticles within this porous carbon structure is expected to enhance mass transport and surface site exposure. Furthermore, confining Co within the CD assemblies is expected to enhance the stability and reusability of the catalytic system for antibiotic degradation, an area that remains underexplored.

In this study, we synthesized three-dimensional (3D) Co-carbon sponge catalysts composed of spatially confined metallic Co and a 3D carbon sponge. The surface functional groups present in the CDs facilitated the anchoring of Co ions, leading to the formation of 3D Co-carbon sponge composites after thermal treatment. The degradation performance of Co@CDs-x (x=800, 900 and 1000) samples was tested using TC as a model contaminant. This study emphasizes the preparation of high crystallinity Co@CDs composites through high-temperature calcination, which effectively prevents agglomeration of Co nanoparticles. The incorporation of CDs led to the formation of Co@CDs composites with a significant specific surface area and mesoporous structure, enhancing the availability of catalytically active sites and mass transport during degradation process. The optimal Co@CDs-900 catalysts exhibited superior performance in activating PMS to generate ROS for TC degradation. To probe the potential application, the reusability of this catalyst, the universality of the catalytic system, and the influences of different anions, organic matter, and solution pH were investigated. Analysis was conducted on the active components of catalyst, active substances in the catalytic system, and degradation intermediates for TC. Furthermore, the acute and chronic toxicity as well as phytotoxicity of the degradation products of TC were finally investigated.
The chemicals and characterization were illustrated in Chapter S1-S2 in Supplementary information.

The CDs were synthesized through a rapid and straightforward method. Initially, acetaldehyde (40 mL) was slowly combined with NaOH (12 g) and stirred for 2 hours until the reaction was complete. The resulting product was then sonicated with dilute HCl until flocculation was observed. Subsequently, the product underwent washing cycles with water until the filtrate reached neutral and was then dried at 70 °C in an oven. Fig. S1 shows the fluorescence emission spectroscopy of self-prepared CDs. The excitation and emission spectra showed that the excitation wavelength of CDs was 467 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength was 573 nm, respectively. The CDs yield was calculated to be 15.66 %. These results confirm the successful synthesis of CDs. To synthesize the 3D porous sponge-like Co@CDs-T materials, 0.075 g cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and 0.1 g CDs were mixed in 25 mL ethanol under stirring. The solvent was evaporated at 80 ℃ to form the precursor, which was then placed in a ceramic boat and calcined at 900 °C with Ar flow for 2 hours, resulting in the production of Co@CDs-900. Similarly, the honeycomb-like Co@CDs-800 and vesicles-like Co@CDs-1000 were prepared using the same method, but with different calcination temperatures (800 and 1000 °C).

The catalytic properties of Co@CDs-T for TC degradation through activating PMS were evaluated by conducting a series of batch experiments. Initially, Co@CDs-T (8 mg) and TC solution (50 mL, 20 mg/L) were combined into a 100 mL beaker and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 15 minutes to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 2.0 mM PMS was quickly added to the beaker, initiating TC degradation. At a specified time, 1.0 mL of the mixture was extracted, and rapidly filtrated using a 0.22 µm membrane. Filtered liquid was mixed homogeneously with 2 mL of methanol. The impact of pH on TC degradation was investigated by adjusting the initial solution pH using H2SO4 or NaOH with a concentration of 1 M. The reusability of Co@CDs-900 was studied by conducting cycling experiments through consecutive cycles. At the end of each cycle, the catalyst was separated using a magnet, washed with distilled water, and then reused for the next run. Each experiment was conducted at least three times to obtain the standard error.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: A range of sponge catalysts, denoted as Co@CDs-T (T = 800, 900, 1000), were prepared by pyrolyzing the cobalt nitrate and CDs complexes with precise control over the calcination temperature ( Fig. 1a). The morphological and microstructural features of the typical Co@CDs-T materials were analyzed. Scanning electron microscopy

(SEM) image revealed a spongy porous structure on the catalyst surface (Fig. 1b and Fig. S2). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image demonstrated an even distribution of Co nanoparticles on the surface of the CD assemblies (Fig. 1c). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images exhibited lattice spacings of 0.20 nm and 0.34 nm (Fig. 1d and Fig. S3), corresponding to the Co (111) and graphitic carbon (002) planes, respectively [32]. These findings indicate that the Co nanoparticles could serve as catalysts, facilitating the formation of porous carbon sponges and promoting carbon graphitization. Additionally, the Co nanoparticles were encapsulated by graphitic carbon layers, which enhance the stability of the Co nanoparticles during the catalytic process. Furthermore, elemental mappings illustrated a uniform distribution of C, Co, and O throughout the entire material (Fig. 1e-g).

X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) indicated that Co@CDs-800, Co@CDs-900, and Co@CDs-1000 displayed identical diffraction peaks at 44.2°, 51.5° and 75.8° ( Fig. 2a), corresponding to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of metallic Co (PDF#15–0806), respectively [32,33]. The Co diffraction peaks were sharp and intense at the calcination temperature of 900 ℃, indicating the highly crystalline nature. Nitrogen sorption isotherms of Co@CDs-800, Co@CDs-900, and Co@CDs-1000 were examined to assess their textural properties. The isotherms displayed IV-type curves with H4-type hysteresis loops, suggesting the presence of both microporous and mesoporous pores in these materials (Fig. 2b and Fig. S4) [34]. Co@CDs-900 exhibited a higher specific surface area (SSA) of 151.063 m2/g compared with Co@CDs-800 (132.877 m2/g), indicating that higher temperatures could lead to an increase in SSA. However, at 1000 °C, the structure collapsed and macropores deteriorated, resulting in a decline in SSA to 125.545 m2/g (Table S1 and Fig. S1). Additionally, the pore volume of Co@CDs-900 (0.164 cm3/g) was larger than that of Co@CDs-800 (0.134 cm3/g) and Co@CDs-1000 (0.133 cm3/g). The Co@CDs-900 sample possessed the largest SSA, offering more sites for metal atoms attachment and enhancing PMS activation towards TC destruction [35]. The broader pore size distribution and larger pore volume are beneficial for mass/ion transport, thereby enhancing the catalyst’s performance [36]. The magnetic property of Co@CDs-900 was investigated using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Fig. 2c). The saturation magnetization strength of Co@CDs-900 was about 41 emu g−1, enabling magnetic recovery of Co@CDs-900 after the degradation process (Fig. 2c inset) [33]. Fig. 2d illustrates the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of the samples. Specifically, the bands observed at 3449, 1600, and 1107 cm−1 corresponded to O-H, CO and C-O telescopic vibrations [20,37,38]. Compared with Co@CDs-800, the intensity of the O-H stretching vibration in Co@CDs-900 decreased, suggesting the removal of the O-H group with increasing calcination temperature [39]. Fig. 2e presents the Raman spectra of the three samples, with peaks at 1340 and 1580 cm−1 were attributed to the D-band of defective carbon and the G-band of graphitized carbon [40,41]. Among the samples, Co@CDs-1000 had an ID/IG value of 0.62, which was lower than that of Co@CDs-800 (0.77) and Co@CDs-900 (0.74), indicating the increasing graphitization degree as the pyrolysis temperature increased [32]. Fig. 2f displays the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum of the typical Co@CDs-900 sample, revealing the presence of C, O and Co elements on the surface. The high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2g) shows peaks at 284.8, 285.9, 286.7, and 288.9 eV corresponding to CC, C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively [42,43]. In the HR-XPS of Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 2h), peaks at 779.5 and 795.9 eV are attributed to Co0 2p 3/2 and Co0 2p 1/2 [39,44], while peaks at 781.8, 797.3, and 802.7 eV are assigned to Co2+ 2p 3/2, Co2+ 2p 1/2, and a satellite peak, respectively [32,45]. The Co0 content was found to be lower than Co2+ due to the surface oxidation of small Co nanoparticles. The HR-XPS of O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2i) exhibits peaks at 531.4, 532.2, 533.3, and 529.8 eV corresponding to CO, C–OH, C–O, and Co–O, respectively [42,46]. Interestingly, Co@CDs-900 (31.17 %) had a higher content of CO compared to Co@CDs-800 (25.35 %) and Co@CDs-1000 (20.68 %) (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6). Given the catalytic role of CO sites in PMS activation, it is anticipated that Co@CDs-900 may exhibit improved degradation efficiency [47].

Fig. 3a demonstrates that PMS alone did not degrade TC, and the adsorption of TC by Co@CDs-900 within 11 minutes was also minimal (rate constant: 0.011 min−1), indicating its relatively low static adsorption capacity. The removal efficiency and rate constant of CDs/PMS for TC were 25 % and 0.022 min−1, respectively. The results showed that CDs exhibited low catalytic activity and primarily served as a platform for dispersing Co nanoparticles. Additionally, CDs possessed a large specific surface area, leading to an increase in the overall specific surface area of the catalyst. This enhances the active sites of the catalyst, ultimately improving the catalytic performance of Co@CDs-900. These findings align with the outcomes of the BET tests and subsequent experimental investigations. Subsequently, we evaluated the performance of Co@CDs-800, Co@CDs-900 and Co@CDs-1000 catalysts to activate PMS for TC degradation. The Co@CDs-900/PMS system demonstrated a high TC removal efficiency of 97.8 % in 11 minutes, achieving a rate constant (k) that was 28 and 39 times greater than those of PMS and Co@CDs-900 alone, respectively (Fig. 3b). The k values for Co@CDs-800/PMS, Co@CDs-900/PMS, and Co@CDs-1000/PMS were 0.277, 0.313, and 0.299 min−1. Notably, Co@CDs-900/PMS showed significant advantages over the reported catalyst/PMS system in degrading TC (Table S2). We also performed first and second order kinetic studies of the process of degradation of TC by Co@CDs-800, Co@CDs-900, and Co@CDs-1000 [48–50]. All kinetic parameters are summarized in Table S3. The optimal dosages of catalyst and PMS applied for TC destruction are shown in Figs. 3c-d and 3e-f. Additionally, the capability of Co@CDs-900/PMS to degrade TC at different temperatures was investigated. The degradation rate of TC exhibited an increase with rising temperature ( Fig. 4a), with the k value rising from 0.249 to 0.322 min−1 as the reaction temperature increased from 15 to 30 °C. These results suggest that Co@CDs-900/PMS is effective in TC removal across a range of temperatures. Generally, PMS activation is more efficient at elevated temperatures, leading to increased ROS generation and enhanced TC removal. Additionally, TC degradation catalyzed by the Co@CDs-900/PMS system adhered to first-order kinetics (Fig. 4b), with a calculated activation energy (Ea) of 13 kJ/mol, indicating a substantial reduction in the energy barrier (Fig. S7).

The assessment of TC degradation on Co@CDs-900/PMS was conducted across a pH range of 2.98–10.97, recognizing the pivotal influence of pH in the generation of free radicals during PMS activation [41]. Fig. 4c illustrates that TC degradation (80.9 % ∼ 96.7 %) increased with rising pH values (2.98 ∼ 10.97). The k value exhibited a volcano trend as the initial pH was raised from 2.98–10.97 (Fig. 4d). Under strongly acidic conditions (pH = 2.98), the degradation rate slightly decreased, likely due to an excess of H+ ions reacting with PMS, which diminishes the oxidation potential and may lead to the depletion of •OH and SO4 •− (Eqs. S3–4) [51–54]. However, TC removal improved as pH increased pH to 10.97. PMS remained highly active under strongly alkaline conditions, potentially due to •OH acting as active sites that enhance electron transfer and PMS dissociation [55,56]. Overall, the findings suggest that TC degradation using Co@CDs-900/PMS can occur over a broad pH range (2.98 ∼ 10.97). The degradation of TC was investigated in the presence of various inorganic anions (Cl−, H2PO4 −, NO3 −, and HCO3 −) and humic acid (HA) ( Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b shows that there was a slight decrease in TC removal efficiency and the k value with increasing Cl− concentrations, which can be attributed to the reaction between Cl− and SO4 •−/•OH radicals. This reaction results in the production of low catalytic active species including Cl•, Cl2•−and HOCl•− (Eqs. S5–7) [57,58]. Furthermore, Fig. 5c shows that TC removal with Co@CDs-900/PMS was slightly inhibited at lower H2PO4 − concentration, while the degradation rate of TC was accelerated at higher H2PO4 − concentrations. This observation aligns with the findings of Peng and colleagues, who reported that even at a H2PO4 − concentration of 10 mM, the degradation of TC was significantly promoted. They attributed this enhancement to the synergistic activation of PMS by H2PO4 −, which further improved TC removal efficiency [59–61]. The degradation efficiency of TC remained above 90 % across different concentrations of NO3 − (Fig. 5d). The degradation was slightly inhibited could be due to the reaction between NO3 − and SO4 •−/•OH radicals, leading to the formation of the lower oxidation state substance NO3 • (Eq. S8). Additionally, as the concentration of HCO3 − increased from 1 mM to 5 mM, the removal efficiency of TC decreased from 96.29 % to 87.46 % (Fig. 5e), indicating a notable inhibitory effect. This inhibition is attributed to the ability of HCO3 − to scavenge SO4 •− and 1O2, resulting in the formation of low-potential carbonate radicals [62,63]. Furthermore, variations in the pH of the solution after the addition of different amounts of HCO3 − were also observed (Fig. S8). As the concentration of HCO3 − increased to 5 mM, the degradation efficiency decreased while the pH showed a slight rise. This phenomenon may be explained by the facile hydrolysis of HCO3 −, which produces OH−, thereby elevating the solution’s pH and diminishing the removal rate of TC [61]. The degradation performance of Co@CDs-900/PMS with different doses of HA was investigated. Fig. 5f shows that the presence of different concentrations of HA exerted a slight inhibitory effect on the oxidative destruction of TC. As the HA dosage increased slightly, the TC removal rate slightly reduced from 91.2 % to 87.6 %, with decreasing k value from 0.219 to 0.179 min−1. The reduction in TC removal efficiency in the presence HA can be assigned to competitive adsorption of HA on the catalyst surface, which obstructs the catalytic surface and hinders the formation of ROS [64].

The reusability of Co@CDs-900/PMS for degrading TC was evaluated over five consecutive runs. Although the removal rate of TC slightly reduced as the recycling runs increased ( Figs. 6a and 6b), Co@CDs-900/PMS demonstrated the high ability to be reused five times for TC degradation. Degradation efficiency remained 81.6 % after the fifth cycle. SEM image revealed that the catalyst maintained its porous structure even after five cycles, indicating its good stability (Fig. 6c). While the slight disruption of the porous structure in the process of repeated cycles could be responsible for the activity loss of Co@CDs-900 [65]. The quantity of Co leaching was assessed using inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), revealing that the amount of Co leaching throughout the repeated cycles was only 0.41 mg/L. This value was below the Chinese wastewater discharge standard (GB 25467–2010) of 1 mg/L[66]. Consequently, the activity loss of the catalyst during reusability testing was ascribed to the slight disruption of the porous structure and Co leaching. Meanwhile, we performed cycling experiments on Co@CDs-800, Co@CDs-900 and Co@CDs-1000. The degradation performance of TC was drastically reduced to 41 % and 46 % after 5 cycles, respectively (Fig. S9). The results showed that Co@CDs-800 and Co@CDs-1000 were less stable than Co@CDs-900, which further confirms that the Co@CDs-900 is the optimal catalyst for activating PMS towards TC removal.

To investigate the generalizability of the Co@CDs-900/PMS system, we selected a variety of organic pollutants including ciprofloxacin (CIP), oxytetracycline (OTC), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), methyl orange (MO), and rhodamine B (RhB). The removal rates of CIP, OTC and 4-NP by Co@CDs-900/PMS within 11 minutes were 85.9 %, 85.9 % and 97.1 % (Fig. S10), respectively. Additionally, Co@CDs-900/PMS system degraded MO and RhB by 97.7 % and 98.9 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the universal applicability of Co@CDs-900/PMS system for a wide range of organic pollutants. The mineralization capacity of Co@CDs-900 was assessed by using total organic carbon (TOC). Fig. S11 demonstrated that Co@CDs-900 effectively activated PMS and efficiently mineralized TC. The mineralization rate gradually increased with degradation and reached 62.26 % within 100 minutes, further confirming the good catalytic performance of Co@CDs-900.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of ROS and activation mechanism of PMS by Co@CDs-900
ce:section.ce:para: Quenching experiments were conducted to investigate the ROS involved in TC degradation. The scavengers including MeOH, TBA, β-carotene, and CF are utilized to deactivate SO4 •−/•OH, •OH, 1O2, and O2 •− [67–69]. Figs. 7a and 7b demonstrate that the degradation rate of TC and the k value decreased to 60.7 % and 0.074 min−1 respectively with 0.5 M MeOH. These results reveal that both •OH and SO4 •− involved in TC degradation catalyzed by Co@CDs-900/PMS. The specific contribution of SO4 •− and •OH was further determined by comparing the removal rate of TC with the addition of TBA [33]. The inhibition of TC degradation by TBA was 4.5 % (Fig. 7a), while the total inhibition of TC degradation by SO4 •− and •OH was 37.1 %. This suggests that the contribution of •OH to TC degradation was less than that of SO4 •−. The contribution ratios of •OH and SO4 •− were calculated to be 22.72 % and 36.29 %, respectively (Table S4 and Fig. 7c). The addition of CF, a typical O2 •− scavenger, had a minimal effect on TC, resulting in a low contribution ratio of 9.89 %. β-Carotene, which is resistant to oxidation by PMS, was used as a typical 1O2 scavenger for quenching tests [69]. The inhibition of TC degradation by β-carotene was measured at 12.51 % (k=0.187 min−1), with the contribution ratio of 1O2 determined to be 31.1 %. To confirm the presence of ROS in the Co@CDs-900/PMS system, EPR tests were conducted using 5, 5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-n-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP) as spin trapping agents. DMPO is typically used to trap •OH, SO4 •−, and O2 •− [39], while TEMP is utilized for 1O2 trapping. The presence of a characteristic triple peak with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 indicated the presence of 1O2 [70] (Fig. 7d). Generally, 1O2 is generated through the interaction of dissolved oxygen and/or PMS dissociation [70]. A comparison experiment with N2 addition was performed to investigate the origins of 1O2 in the Co@CDs-900/PMS system (Fig. S12), revealing minimal effects on TC decomposition and suggesting that 1O2 generation primarily arises from PMS decomposition. Additionally, a distinct signal of DMPO-SO4 •− was observed, indicating the existence of both •OH and SO4 •− [71] (Fig. 7e). No significant signals for DMPO-O2 •− were detected [20,72](Fig. 7f), indicating that O2 •− was not the primary ROS responsible for TC removal, which is consistent with the results from the CF bursting experiment. In summary, SO4 •−, •OH, O2 •−, and 1O2 were involved in TC degradation; however, SO4 •− and 1O2 appear to be the dominant ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of active sites of Co@CDs-900 for PMS activation
ce:section.ce:para:
XPS spectra of Co 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s for the reacted Co@CDs-900 were analyzed to study the catalytic active sites of Co@CDs-900 towards activating PMS to generate ROS for TC removal (Fig. S13). The Co 2p spectrum showed a decrease in the content of Co0 from 19.23 % to 7.87 % and Co2+ from 44.98 % to 37.58 %, indicating a cyclic relationship among Co0, Co2+ and Co3+ (Eqs. (1)-(3)) [44]. The O 1 s spectrum revealed a decrease in the relative amount of CO from 31.17 % to 28.33 %. This confirms that CO is the active substance involved in the activation of PMS to generate the non-radical 1O2. In the C 1 s spectra, the relative content of CC reduced from 78.97 % to 69.13 % after the reaction, indicating that localised electron on sp2 hybridised carbon facilitates the dissociation of O-O bonds of PMS to produce ROS and promote TC destruction (Eqs. 4–5) [44,46]. In an aqueous solution, SO4 •− radicals can react with OH-/H2O in a radical interconversion to produce •OH radicals (Eqs. 6–7) [73]. Additionally, 1O2 can be produced by PMS self-breaking [73], and SO5 •− can self-react to form S2O8 2-, SO4 2− and 1O2 (Eqs. 8–12) [46,67]. Ultimately, these active species can oxidize TC molecules to intermediate products, CO2 and H2O (Eq. 13). Based on this analysis, a plausible mechanism for TC degradation over Co@CDs-900/PMS is proposed ( Fig. 8).
(1) Co0 + 2HSO5 − → Co2+ + 2SO4 •− + 2OH−
(2) Co2+ + HSO5 − → Co3+ + OH− + SO4 •−
(3) Co3+ + HSO5 − → Co2+ + SO5 •− + H+
(4) C=C + HSO5 − → C=C+ + SO4 •− + OH−
(5) C=C+ + HSO5 − → C=C + SO5 •− + H+
(6) SO4 •− + OH− → •OH + SO4 2−
(7) SO4 •− + H2O → SO4 2− + •OH + H+
(8) HSO5 − → SO5 2− + H+
(9) HSO5 − + SO5 2− → 1O2 + 2SO4 2− + H+
(10) HSO5 − + SO4 •− → SO5 •− + SO4 2− + H+
(11) SO5 2− + SO5 2− → S2O8 2- + 1O2
(12) SO5 2− + SO5 2− → 2SO4 2− + 1O2
(13) SO4 •− /•OH /1O2/ O2 •− + TC → intermediate products + CO2 + H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathways of TC and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the possible degradation pathways, the intermediates of TC decomposition by Co@CDs-900/PMS were characterized by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Fig. S14). Three main degradation pathways were identified based on the mass spectra ( Fig. 9), and the possible intermediates of the degradation process were summarized in Table S5. Pathway I involved multiple hydroxylation processes, resulting in the formation of P1. P1 subsequently underwent dehydration and deamidation to yield P2, which then experienced a ring-opening reaction to produce P3. These intermediates were further oxidized into the small-molecule organic substance P4, which was partially mineralized into CO2 and H2O [20]. In pathway II, TC was initially attacked by 1O2, forming the ketone compound P1, which was then oxidized by 1O2 to P5, resulting in the loss of amides, methyls, and hydroxyls. Further oxidation and decomposition led to the formation of P6 [74–76], subsequently oxidized and decomposed to P7 under continuous attack by ROS. Pathway III involved the oxidation of TC, producing P8 with a higher molecular weight, which then underwent deamination and removal reactions to yield P12. Ultimately, P12 was degraded into small molecules, where were finally converted into H2O and CO2 [77,78].

To investigate the toxicity of TC degradation, mung bean seeds were incubated in a degraded TC solution for seven days, and the germination length was calculated. For comparison, mung bean seeds were also incubated in the original TC solution and in pure water (Fig. S15). The average lengths of mung bean seeds germinated in pure water, degraded TC solution, and undegraded TC solution were 18.9 cm, 13.3 cm, and 8.5 cm, respectively (Table S6). These findings suggest that TC degradation using Co@CDs-900/PMS effectively reduces its toxicity towards plants. However, the treated solution still exhibited inhibitory effects on mung bean growth. This may be attributed to Co ion leaching or the presence of intermediate products that adversely affect the growth of mung beans [44]. To assess the toxicity of TC and its degradation intermediates, the ECOSAR program was used to predict their acute and chronic toxicity [33]. The toxicity levels of these compounds are categorized accordingly to the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS). The results presented in Table S7 indicate that TC displays varying degrees of toxicity across various species. For acute toxicity, TC is harmful to daphnia and algae, but not to fish. However, in the context of chronic toxicity, Daphnia emerges as the most susceptible species to both TC and its intermediate products, with certain intermediates showing enhanced ChV values towards Daphnia. The intermediates P1, P2, and P12 demonstrates increased toxicity compared to TC. In the case of fish, some intermediates (P2, P12) remain highly toxic, while others exhibit reduced toxicity levels. Likewise, certain intermediates like P2 demonstrate higher toxicity to algae than TC. Overall, Co@CDs-900/PMS effectively reduces the toxicity of TC after degradation. The harmful intermediates mentioned above are eventually mineralized into small, non-polluting molecules.

We have developed a novel 3D porous spongy magnetic metal nanocarbon catalyst using Co-CDs as precursors. The results revealed that Co nanoparticles were formed and encapsulated within carbon layers, effectively preventing excessive metal ion leaching. The synthesized Co@CDs-900 exhibited excellent catalytic performance in degrading TC through PMS activation, achieving a degradation rate of 97.8 % within 11 minutes. Additionally, the Co@CDs-900/PMS system efficiently degraded TC with HA, various inorganic anions and different solution pH levels. The magnetic properties of Co@CDs-900 also aided in separating it from the reaction solution. Even after five cycles of TC degradation, the removal rate remained at 81.5 %. The Co@CDs-900/PMS system exhibited both radical and non-radical degradation pathways, with SO4 •− and 1O2 identified as the primary ROS for TC degradation. Furthermore, the toxicity of the degradation intermediate products was lower than that of the initial TC, indicating the potential of Co@CDs-900 for environmental remediation applications. This study introduces a cost-effective and straightforward method for producing metal-carbon composites aimed at remediating organic pollutants.

Jie Wu: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Guangyin Fan: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yuxi Kang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Qiulin Li: Formal analysis.
